nativeRange.habitat: Coastal to outer reef crests and slopes with rich invertebrate growth; generally found at depths of 0–60 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on coral tips (particularly Acropora), crustaceans, molluscs, sponges, tunicates, and algae in the wild. In captivity, tends to accept a varied meaty diet including squid, krill, clams, and hard-shelled shrimp. Hard-shelled prey such as clams and crabs are generally advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like teeth.

summary: A personable Indo-Pacific puffer with a dog-like face, reaching around 33 cm; generally considered semi-aggressive and best kept singly in a large fish-only system, where it tends to become notably interactive with its keeper.
careNotes:
Arothron nigropunctatus is distributed throughout the Indo-Pacific from East Africa to Micronesia and Samoa, north to southern Japan, south to New South Wales, inhabiting reef crests and slopes with rich invertebrate growth at 0–60 m depth.

This species displays notable color polymorphism: individuals may appear grey-brown with scattered black spots, a half-yellow phase with yellow on the lower body, or occasionally an all-yellow phase. Color-phase transitions are rarely observed in captivity. The 'dog face' common name refers to the rounded, blunt snout and large expressive eyes that give the fish a canine appearance.

Like other Arothron puffers, this species has a beak-like structure formed by fused teeth that grows continuously throughout life; offering hard-shelled foods such as clams and crab legs is generally advised to prevent dental overgrowth. It lacks pelvic fins and uses its pectoral, dorsal, and anal fins for locomotion.

Newly introduced specimens tend to be shy, but the species has been known to become increasingly outgoing over time and is often described as interactive and recognizing its keeper. This behavioural transition can take days to weeks.

Minimum tank size is a subject of significant disagreement across sources: hobby-oriented retailers recommend approximately 150 US gallons (568 L), while specialist and scientific sources suggest up to 1,500 L to adequately accommodate a fully grown 33 cm specimen with open swimming space. The value used here (700 L) is biased toward the cautious larger end of the hobby recommendation. Aquarists are advised to provide as much space as practical.

The species is not reef-safe and will consume corals (particularly Acropora tips), crustaceans, molluscs, and sponges. It is best kept in a fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) system with robust tankmates of similar or greater size.

Like all Tetraodontidae, this species contains tetrodotoxin and is highly toxic to eat. Under significant stress, the species may release skin toxins into the water; heavy filtration and a robust protein skimmer are generally advised. Transfer using a container rather than a net is recommended, as the species may inhale air while out of water — air ingestion can be fatal. Breeding has not been documented in captivity.

Temperature recommendations differ between hobby sources (LiveAquaria: 22.2–25.6°C / 72–78°F) and wild-data scientific sources (FishBase: 24.7–29.3°C). The range used here (23–27°C) represents an average across sources. Salinity figures range from 1.020–1.025 (LiveAquaria) to approximately 1.026 (ReefApp); the range used here (1.020–1.026) spans the reported spread.
breedingNotes: Breeding has not been documented in captivity. The species is oviparous in the wild. No captive breeding protocols are reported in the available literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Arothron nigropunctatus, Bloch & Schneider 1801), taxonomy (Tetraodontidae / Tetraodontiformes), maximum length (33 cm TL), native range (Indo-Pacific: East Africa to Micronesia and Samoa, north to southern Japan, south to New South Wales), temperature range (24.7–29.3°C from wild data), depth range (0–60 m), habitat (coastal to outer reef crest and slopes with rich invertebrate growth), and diet (corals, crustaceans, mollusks, sponges, tunicates, algae). Confirmed poisonous to eat (tetrodotoxin). IUCN Least Concern (2011).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (33 cm / 13 in), minimum tank size (1,500 L — significantly higher than LiveAquaria's 568 L hobby recommendation), salinity (~SG 1.026), temperament (mostly peaceful but threat to invertebrates), diet, and reef safety (not reef-safe). Wide tank-size disagreement (568 L vs 1,500 L, >2x) documented in careNotes; dataStatus set to needs_review per playbook step 3. Final tank value of 700 L is biased toward the cautious larger side from the nice-values list.

fish.breedingNotes: Not yet documented in captivity. Oviparous in the wild.
